control methods: Agricultural prevention and control: planting disease resistant varieties. When cleaning the orchard, remove branches, fallen leaves, and dead fruits with diseases and burn them together. Strengthen cultivation management, enhance tree vigor, and improve disease resistance. Chemical control: combined with the prevention and control of other fruit rot diseases, spray 40% Da Fu Dan wettable powder (450-500 times liquid), or 40% Ke Jun Dan wettable powder (450-500 times liquid), or 40% Sha De Dan wettable powder (450-500 times liquid), or 50% mixed sulfur suspension (500 times liquid), or 36% methyl thiophanate suspension (500-600 times liquid), or 50% benomyl wettable powder (1500 times liquid) from the germination stage. Spray once every 10-14 days for 2-3 consecutive times.

Eliminate the source of the disease: thoroughly cut down cypress trees within a 5-kilometer radius of the orchard, as they are the main host of rust bacteria. Newly built orchards should be at least 5 kilometers away from cypress planting areas. Existing cypress trees in orchards should be removed or isolated to prevent pathogen spread. Chemical control: for cypress, check for fungal galls and 'gum flowers' in winter and spring, remove and destroy them, and spray 50% methyl tobramycin (600-fold solution) or 50% carbendazim (600-fold solution) before the end of March. For apple trees, spray 5 waves of carbofuran sulfur compound before sprouting. Spray 2-3 times every 10-15 days from leaf development to young fruit stage (thumb cap size) with fungicides such as mancozeb, fluazithromycin, and benzimidazole. Spray systemic fungicides (e.g., tebuconazole, triazolone) within 5 days after rain or cypress flowering. Agricultural prevention and control: reasonable pruning to improve ventilation and light, reduce humidity; choose disease-resistant varieties and avoid dense planting; establish protective forest belts to isolate pathogens. Biological control: regularly inspect and destroy fungal galls on cypress trees from winter to spring to reduce pathogens.

Scientific irrigation and fertilization: maintain appropriate soil moisture, avoiding over drying or waterlogging; apply fertilizers reasonably, balancing nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium, avoid excessive nitrogen, and enhance plant disease resistance. Trimming and ventilation: regularly trim branches and leaves to maintain ventilation and light in the tree crown; remove diseased and overly dense branches to reduce pathogen breeding. Health management: timely clean fallen leaves, fruit residues, and surrounding weeds to reduce overwintering and breeding grounds; keep trimming, harvesting, and other tools clean to avoid spreading pathogens. Regular monitoring and observation: check leaves, fruits, and branches weekly to detect early pests and diseases; use insect traps or sticky boards to monitor pest populations and take preventive measures. Biology and preventive control: apply biological agents (e.g., Bacillus subtilis, Rhizopus oryzae) to enhance plant immunity; during high incidence periods, spray low-toxicity preventive pesticides, avoiding healthy leaves. Principles of protecting healthy leaves: prioritize prevention, maintain healthy leaves, avoid blind pesticide use, reduce environmental pollution and pesticide residues, and focus on comprehensive management to maintain plant vitality and disease resistance.

Agricultural prevention and control: reasonable pruning to ensure good orchard permeability, reducing black spot disease; after winter pruning, control branches per mu at around 80000; after spring sprouting, remove useless sprouts near cuts; in summer, prune multiple branches to maintain uniaxial extension; in autumn, remove excess large branches to prevent canopy closure. Maintain strong tree vigor and improve disease resistance by increasing fertilizer and water, controlling fruit yield, and other measures. Garden cleaning: in winter, clean fallen leaves and remove weeds; in spring, scrape off branch ulcers and disease spots; spray 5 waves of Meidu stone sulfur mixture during bud dew red period to reduce bacteria and disease occurrence. Chemical control: in early May, spray 10% multi-antifungal WP (1000-2000 times), 10% agricultural antibiotic 120 WP (800 times), 40% DuPont Fuxing emulsion (8000-10000 times), PPK (800 times), Longdeng Fulian (800-1000 times) and other protective fungicides. After symptoms appear, spray highly permeable, rain-resistant, long-lasting fungicides for therapeutic and eradication effects; during production, use 1000-1200 times Longdeng Fulian solution, 5000-6000 times propiconazole solution, 500-600 times Jundu Qing solution, 1000-1500 times tebuconazole solution, 2000 times Shigao solution, and 1500 times benzodiazepine solution for prevention and control.

Implement crop rotation: rotate crops every 2-3 years with cruciferous vegetables such as non-sweet peppers, chili peppers, and cabbage. Cultivation management: flatten the land; use ridge planting in northern China and high box with deep ditch planting in southern China; ensure timely drainage after rain to prevent water accumulation and flooding. Seed disinfection: before sowing, mix seeds with 0.3% of seed weight of 50% Hujiao fertilizer copper acid WP or 50% Dikesong WP; alternatively, soak seeds in 1% hydrochloric acid solution for 2-3 hours, then rinse with clean water to promote germination. Clean up the countryside: promptly remove diseased and disabled plants or deeply plow after harvesting. Chemical prevention and control: from early disease stage, spray 50% Hujiao fertilizer copper acid WP (500 times), 14% ammonium copper solution (300 times), 77% wettable particle powder (400-500 times), 1:1:200 Bordeaux mixture, 72% agricultural streptomycin sulfate soluble powder, or streptomycin sulfate (4000 times), every 7-10 days for 2-3 continuous applications.

Trimming and ventilation: regularly trim branches and leaves to maintain ventilation and light in the tree crown; remove diseased and overly dense branches to reduce pathogen breeding. Health management: timely clean fallen leaves, fruit residues, and surrounding weeds to reduce overwintering and breeding grounds; keep trimming, harvesting, and other tools clean to avoid spreading pathogens. Regular monitoring and observation: check leaves, fruits, and branches weekly to detect early pests and diseases; use insect traps or sticky boards to monitor pest populations and take preventive measures. Biology and preventive control: apply biological agents (e.g., Bacillus subtilis, Rhizopus oryzae) to enhance plant immunity; during high incidence periods, spray low-toxicity preventive pesticides, avoiding healthy leaves. Principles of protecting healthy leaves: prioritize prevention, maintain healthy leaves, avoid blind pesticide use, reduce environmental pollution and pesticide residues, and focus on comprehensive management to maintain plant vitality and disease resistance.

Chemical control: preventive pesticide application 10-15 days before first flowering using 70% methyl tobramycin (800 times solution) and 25% ether fungicide (1500 times solution) to prevent diseases such as powdery mildew. High-efficiency pesticides: 2.5% cyhalothrin (2000 times solution for beetle and pear moth) + 10% imidacloprid (1500 times solution for green bug), best applied in the evening. Biological pesticides: Bacillus subtilis, Trichoderma, etc., to inhibit pathogen growth, suitable for early disease stages. Field management: reasonable planting to maintain ventilation and light, avoid biased nitrogen application; clean up diseased plants and leaves before leaf fall, burn or bury them; nutritional regulation by spraying 0.05%-0.1% borax solution (dissolved in hot water) to enhance flower pot stress resistance. Climate adaptation: during rainy weather, shorten interval between protective fungicide applications to 5-7 days; during high temperature drying, add organic silicon additives to enhance fungicide permeability.

Scientific irrigation and fertilization: maintain appropriate soil moisture, avoiding over drying or waterlogging; apply fertilizers reasonably, balancing nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium, avoid excessive nitrogen, and enhance plant disease resistance. Trimming and ventilation: regularly trim branches and leaves to maintain ventilation and light in the tree crown; remove diseased and overly dense branches to reduce pathogen breeding. Health management: timely clean fallen leaves, fruit residues, and surrounding weeds to reduce overwintering and breeding grounds; keep trimming, harvesting, and other tools clean to avoid spreading pathogens. Regular monitoring and observation: check leaves, fruits, and branches weekly to detect early pests and diseases; use insect traps or sticky boards to monitor pest populations and take preventive measures. Biology and preventive control: apply biological agents (e.g., Bacillus subtilis, Rhizopus oryzae) to enhance plant immunity; during high incidence periods, spray low-toxicity preventive pesticides, avoiding healthy leaves. Principles of protecting healthy leaves: prioritize prevention, maintain healthy leaves, avoid blind pesticide use, reduce environmental pollution and pesticide residues, and focus on comprehensive management to maintain plant vitality and disease resistance.

Agricultural prevention and control: variety selection by breeding disease-resistant and medium/late maturing varieties; crop rotation with non-leguminous crops for more than 2 years; clean up the countryside after harvesting by removing diseased and residual plants, centralized burning or deep burial, and deep plowing to reduce overwintering pathogens; strengthen field management through deep ditch and high ridge cultivation, reasonable and dense planting, scientific fertilization with organic and phosphorus-potassium fertilizers, timely pruning, open drainage systems, and timely drainage after rain. Chemical control: for summer corn rust, start spraying in early disease stages, once every 7-10 days for 1-2 continuous applications. Green prevention and treatment drugs include 20% benzofenapyr microemulsion (Jie Jun) 1500-2000 times dilution (30-60 g/acre), 30% Fluconazole microemulsion (Shifei) 300-5000 times dilution (12-15 g/acre), 40% Fluconazole (Fuxing) emulsion 6000-8000 times dilution (7.5-10 g/acre), 18% tebuconazole microemulsion (Anying) 1000-2000 times dilution (50-100 g/acre), 10% Chlorpromazine WDG (Shigao) 800-1200 times dilution (50-75 g/acre), 43% tebuconazole suspension (Holick) 3000-4000 times (15-20 g/acre), 75% oxime bacteria · tebuconazole WDG 3000 times (25-35 g/acre). Conventional prevention drugs include 80% mancozeb WP 600-800 times (75-100 g/acre) and other spray treatments.

Plant resistant hybrids: choose corn varieties bred for resistance to leaf spot diseases. Crop rotation: rotate corn with non-host crops to help break the disease cycle. Residue management: bury or remove crop residue where fungi overwinter to reduce inoculum. Fungicides: apply fungicides to manage disease spread. Ensure proper airflow: maintain proper plant spacing to improve air circulation and help leaves dry faster, reducing disease development.

Agricultural prevention and control: choose planting sites with suitable humidity and temperature to prevent corn leaf spot disease. Promote disease-resistant varieties, considering resistance as the primary factor in areas prone to the disease; combine and rotate varieties to avoid homogenization. Improve cultivation environment by implementing planned crop rotation to avoid repeated or consecutive planting. Chemical control: remove yellow and diseased leaves at the plant base to reduce reinfection sources, improve ventilation and light. Spray fungicides from late heart lobe to tasseling stage or early disease stage, once every 10 days for 2-3 continuous applications. Recommended fungicides: 50% Haosujing WP (1000 times), 80% Sukejing WP (1000 times), 50% carbendazim WP (500 times), 50% thiophanate methyl WP (600 times), 5% chlorothalonil WP (300 times), 25% benazolin emulsion (800 times), 40% Kewensan emulsion (800-1000 times), Stirling water agent (2500 times). Alternatively, use 50 g/ha 80% mancozeb WP, 1.5 kg/ha 70% methyl tobuzen WP, 1.5 kg/ha 50% carbendazim WP, 1.5 kg/ha 75% chlorothalonil WP, or Streptolin water at 2500 times dilution.

Eliminate overwintering pathogens. Ensure timely drainage and apply organic fertilizer. From June to September, spray 600 times dilution of Baijunqing, 800 times dilution of 50% carbendazim or tobramycin, and 1:1:200 Bordeaux mixture. In southern regions, focus pesticide applications on three key periods: pre-flowering, post-flowering, and fruit growth. Bordeaux mixture is the primary pesticide used.

Tree crown management: improve air circulation by thinning branches, pruning, and removing base leaves to help grape vines dry faster after rain; avoid excessive sun exposure by not removing too many leaves, especially in warm climates; support vines with proper trellis to increase air circulation and reduce diseases. Hygiene and vineyard management: remove infected parts including dried fruits, diseased branches, and ulcers; destroy remains of diseased plants; control weeds by removing them and keeping the vineyard floor clean to improve air circulation, reduce humidity, and inhibit fungal growth. Trimming and wound treatment: prune during dormant winter period, avoid pruning during humid periods; protect fresh wounds with wax, fungicides, or biological products; minimize wound size by leaving a dry cone during pruning and removing it the following year. Chemical control: apply fungicides as protective agents starting when new shoots are 2-4 inches, and reapply when shoots reach 10-15 inches; spray regularly before flowering, after flowering, and after fruiting; use appropriate fungicides such as copper-based fungicides, carbendazim, mancozeb, or other registered products; use autumn sprays during dormancy containing methyl glycyrrhizinate, ketoconazole, or copper-based fungicides.

Scientific irrigation and fertilization: maintain appropriate soil moisture, avoiding over drying or waterlogging; apply fertilizers reasonably, balancing nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium, avoid excessive nitrogen, and enhance plant disease resistance. Trimming and ventilation: regularly trim branches and leaves to maintain ventilation and light in the tree crown; remove diseased and overly dense branches to reduce pathogen breeding. Health management: timely clean fallen leaves, fruit residues, and surrounding weeds to reduce overwintering and breeding grounds; keep trimming, harvesting, and other tools clean to avoid spreading pathogens. Regular monitoring and observation: check leaves, fruits, and branches weekly to detect early pests and diseases; use insect traps or sticky boards to monitor pest populations and take preventive measures. Biology and preventive control: apply biological agents (e.g., Bacillus subtilis, Rhizopus oryzae) to enhance plant immunity; during high incidence periods, spray low-toxicity preventive pesticides, avoiding healthy leaves. Principles of protecting healthy leaves: prioritize prevention, maintain healthy leaves, avoid blind pesticide use, reduce environmental pollution and pesticide residues, and focus on comprehensive management to maintain plant vitality and disease resistance.

Agricultural prevention and control: after winter garden clearing, thoroughly remove diseased and disabled plants and burn them to reduce overwintering bacteria; remove old leaves at the base during the growing season to improve ventilation and light transmission, and trim overly dense branches and vines; control nitrogen fertilizer application, increase phosphorus and potassium fertilizers, and spray 0.3% potassium dihydrogen phosphate solution on leaves to enhance disease resistance; establish drainage channels to prevent high humidity after rain. Chemical control: at the beginning of disease, spray 800 times dilution of 50% carbendazim WP, 1000 times of 70% thiophanate methyl WP, or 600 times of 75% chlorothalonil WP; coordinate prevention and treatment of black spot disease and downy mildew, spraying once every 10-15 days for 3-4 continuous applications; spray 1:0.7:200 Bordeaux mixture before the rainy season as preventive measure.

Manage seedlings carefully: avoid purchasing seedlings carrying diseases and pests, strictly control entry and exit of seedlings, and eliminate pathogen sources at the origin to prevent worsening of orchard diseases and ensure newly established orchards are disease-free. Control citrus psyllids: spray pesticides on contiguous orchards during six key periods—early spring clearing, spring shoot, summer shoot, autumn shoot, late autumn shoot tender shoot period, and winter—to effectively kill citrus psyllids, the main vector of citrus huanglongbing; conduct regular field detection and reporting, and eliminate psyllids before adult and nymph stages. Replan and select new planting areas with good isolation conditions, scientifically control orchard planting area, and construct ecological isolation zones based on forests to hinder psyllid spread.

Agricultural prevention and control: select disease-resistant varieties such as 'Contender'; avoid low-lying areas with water accumulation, ensure good drainage, and maintain orchard ventilation and transparency; thoroughly remove diseased branches and fruits in winter and dispose of them centrally; scrape bark in early spring and apply stone sulfur mixture. Chemical control: before sprouting, spray branches with 100-fold solution of 40% fumarate; 10 days after anthesis, spray 70% thiophanate methyl 800 times solution, 15 days apart; apply 1000-1500 fold solution of chlorobromoisocyanuric acid and 50-fold solution of chloramphenicol acetate to lesions; avoid long-term single use of triazole agents and alternate with protective fungicides such as mancozeb. Biological control: spray Bacillus subtilis after harvesting to form a protective film; spray 500-fold solution of Trichoderma preparation in early disease stages, every 7-10 days. Physical control: bag fruits with double-layer paper 30 days after flowering and spray pyrazole ether fungicide before bagging; use sex traps to lure and kill infectious insects such as peach moth.

Scientific irrigation and fertilization: maintain appropriate soil moisture, avoiding over drying or waterlogging; apply fertilizers reasonably, balancing nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium, avoid excessive nitrogen, and enhance plant disease resistance. Trimming and ventilation: regularly trim branches and leaves to maintain ventilation and light in the tree crown; remove diseased and overly dense branches to reduce pathogen breeding. Health management: timely clean fallen leaves, fruit residues, and surrounding weeds to reduce overwintering and breeding grounds; keep trimming, harvesting, and other tools clean to avoid spreading pathogens. Regular monitoring and observation: check leaves, fruits, and branches weekly to detect early pests and diseases; use insect traps or sticky boards to monitor pest populations and take preventive measures. Biology and preventive control: apply biological agents (e.g., Bacillus subtilis, Rhizopus oryzae) to enhance plant immunity; during high incidence periods, spray low-toxicity preventive pesticides, avoiding healthy leaves. Principles of protecting healthy leaves: prioritize prevention, maintain healthy leaves, avoid blind pesticide use, reduce environmental pollution and pesticide residues, and focus on comprehensive management to maintain plant vitality and disease resistance.

Agricultural prevention and control: choose early maturing, disease-resistant, high-yield potato varieties and harvest timely; strengthen cultivation management by selecting fertile, well-drained fields, applying organic and potassium fertilizers, timely irrigation, and removing diseased and residual plants to reduce initial infection sources; ensure reasonable storage and transportation by harvesting fully ripe potatoes, minimizing damage, avoiding storage of diseased potatoes, maintaining 4 ℃ temperature and proper ventilation; deeply dig the soil and remove diseased seed potatoes; avoid rotation or proximity with Solanaceae crops. Chemical control: before disease onset, alternately spray 25% amisida at 1500-2000 times dilution or 70% mancozeb wettable powder at 600-800 times dilution every 7-10 days for 2-3 consecutive times; after onset, spray 50% suquelin WP (800-1000 times), 70% mancozeb WP (600-800 times), 50% paraquat WP (1000-1500 times), 70% thiophanate methyl WP (800 times), 72% frost urea, and manganese zinc spray (600-800 times), alternating agents every 7 days and spraying 2-3 consecutive times to prevent resistance and improve control efficacy.

Scientific irrigation and fertilization: maintain appropriate soil moisture, avoiding over drying or waterlogging; apply fertilizers reasonably, balancing nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium, avoid excessive nitrogen, and enhance plant disease resistance. Trimming and ventilation: regularly trim branches and leaves to maintain ventilation and light in the tree crown; remove diseased and overly dense branches to reduce pathogen breeding. Health management: timely clean fallen leaves, fruit residues, and surrounding weeds to reduce overwintering and breeding grounds; keep trimming, harvesting, and other tools clean to avoid spreading pathogens. Regular monitoring and observation: check leaves, fruits, and branches weekly to detect early pests and diseases; use insect traps or sticky boards to monitor pest populations and take preventive measures. Biology and preventive control: apply biological agents (e.g., Bacillus subtilis, Rhizopus oryzae) to enhance plant immunity; during high incidence periods, spray low-toxicity preventive pesticides, avoiding healthy leaves. Principles of protecting healthy leaves: prioritize prevention, maintain healthy leaves, avoid blind pesticide use, reduce environmental pollution and pesticide residues, and focus on comprehensive management to maintain plant vitality and disease resistance.

Agricultural prevention and control: select disease-resistant varieties and use disease-free seed potatoes to reduce initial infection sources; increase soil cultivation and ensure good drainage in seed fields to prevent bacterial infiltration and rain-mediated infection; implement reasonable crop rotation with intervals over 3 years, avoiding fields with frequent late blight outbreaks; strengthen field management by choosing loose, well-drained soils, promoting vigorous growth, enhancing disease resistance, and conducting inspections before and after flowering; once central diseased plants are found, remove and deeply bury them along with nearby diseased leaves and disinfect with lime. Chemical control: at the early stage of late blight, remove diseased plants and apply the first spray promptly; spray every 7–10 days for two consecutive applications; available fungicides include 58% metalaxyl-mancozeb, 64% antiviral alum, mancozeb (M-45), 72% ketol, or 69% Anke mancozeb; alternate different fungicide types to prevent resistance development.

Scientific irrigation and fertilization: maintain appropriate soil moisture, avoiding over drying or waterlogging; apply fertilizers reasonably, balancing nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium, avoid excessive nitrogen, and enhance plant disease resistance. Trimming and ventilation: regularly trim branches and leaves to maintain ventilation and light in the tree crown; remove diseased and overly dense branches to reduce pathogen breeding. Health management: timely clean fallen leaves, fruit residues, and surrounding weeds to reduce overwintering and breeding grounds; keep trimming, harvesting, and other tools clean to avoid spreading pathogens. Regular monitoring and observation: check leaves, fruits, and branches weekly to detect early pests and diseases; use insect traps or sticky boards to monitor pest populations and take preventive measures. Principles of protecting healthy leaves: prioritize prevention, maintain healthy leaves, avoid blind pesticide use, reduce environmental pollution and pesticide residues, and focus on comprehensive management to maintain plant vitality and disease resistance.

Agricultural prevention and control includes strengthening cultivation management by adopting high bed planting, reasonable and dense planting to improve ventilation, light transmission, and drainage, enhancing plant growth and disease resistance. In protected cultivation, control watering, ventilate sheds on sunny and cloudy days, and raise midday temperatures to 35 ℃ to suppress disease development. Clean the countryside by timely removal of diseased and old leaves, and after harvest, remove, bury, or burn diseased and damaged plant material and plow the soil to accelerate decomposition. Chemical control involves early foliar sprays with 53.8% solution (2000x), 10% Shigao (1500x), 40% Fuxing (3000x), 72% Prick (3000x), 12.5% Supoli (2000x), 2% plus rice (600x), or 600x sodium bicarbonate solution, applied every 7-10 days for 2-4 times, or every 3-4 days for 4-6 times when 1-2 disease spots appear. Additionally, spray 25% Ethoxypyr Suspension (1500-2500x), 30% Ethoxystrobin Suspension (1500-2500x), 30% Fluconazole Wettable Powder (1500-2000x), 10% Pheniconazole Water Dispersible Granules (1500-2500x), or 25% Triazolone Wettable Powder (2000x) once every 7-10 days for 2-3 consecutive applications for disease prevention and control.

Scientific irrigation and fertilization: maintain appropriate soil moisture, avoiding over drying or waterlogging; apply fertilizers reasonably, balancing nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium, avoid excessive nitrogen, and enhance plant disease resistance. Trimming and ventilation: regularly trim branches and leaves to maintain ventilation and light in the tree crown; remove diseased and overly dense branches to reduce pathogen breeding. Health management: timely clean fallen leaves, fruit residues, and surrounding weeds to reduce overwintering and breeding grounds; keep trimming, harvesting, and other tools clean to avoid spreading pathogens. Regular monitoring and observation: check leaves, fruits, and branches weekly to detect early pests and diseases; use insect traps or sticky boards to monitor pest populations and take preventive measures. Biology and preventive control: apply biological agents (e.g., Bacillus subtilis, Rhizopus oryzae) to enhance plant immunity; during high incidence periods, spray low-toxicity preventive pesticides, avoiding healthy leaves. Principles of protecting healthy leaves: prioritize prevention, maintain healthy leaves, avoid blind pesticide use, reduce environmental pollution and pesticide residues, and focus on comprehensive management to maintain plant vitality and disease resistance.

Remove weeds from the field and surrounding areas before transplanting, and burn or compost them; deeply plow the ground to eliminate weeds, promote decomposition of diseased and disabled bodies, and reduce sources of disease and insects. Choose disease resistant varieties with strong growth potential, high fruit setting rate, cold resistance, shade tolerance, and resistance to gray mold, viral diseases, powdery mildew, and shoot blight; select mother plants from pure, robust, disease-free seedlings with well-developed root systems. Three days before transplantation, spray a mixture of disease and pest control agents, transplant with soil and medicine, and eliminate diseased and weak seedlings during transplantation. Spray a mixture of disease and pest control agents once before covering seedlings with moisture, adding 0.3% urea if growth is weak. Biological control: 2% Nongkang 120 water solution 200x liquid, 90% New Phytomycin soluble powder 4000x solution, 3% Mesomycin wettable powder 1000x solution, 72% agricultural streptomycin sulfate wettable powder 3000-4000 times dilution. Chemical control: Soak seedlings in 500 fold solution of 70% methyl tobramycin wettable powder for 15-20 minutes before transplantation; soil treatment with 11.25 kg of 50% Fumeishuang wettable powder or 40% mixed seed powder per hectare, mixed with 1500 kg fine soil before hole application; spray 30% alkaline copper sulfate suspension 500 fold solution.

Basic methods: Use formula fertilization technology, apply compost from fermented bacteria or fully decomposed organic fertilizer, irrigate reasonably, avoid excessive watering, water the greenhouse in the morning, and release air timely to prevent high humidity. Pay attention to field hygiene, collect and burn disease residues promptly. Pharmaceutical method: In early stages of the disease, spray 400 fold solution of 30% basic copper sulfate (Green Dabao) suspension, 500 fold solution of 50% Hujiao fertilizer copper acid wettable powder, 300 fold solution of 14% ammonium copper water agent, 500 fold solution of 77% wettable powder, 800 fold solution of 56% water dispersible micro granules, and 800-900 fold solution of 47% Jia Rui Nong wettable powder every 10 days, spraying 65 liters per 677m², stopping 3 days before harvesting. Also, spray 36% methyl thiophanate suspension, 50% mixed disinfectant suspension at 500 times, 40% sulfur suspension at 600 times, 50% compound methyl thiophanate wettable powder at 800-900 times, 50% carbendazim wettable powder at 1000 times, and 75% chlorothalonil wettable powder at 1000 times every 10 days for continuous prevention and treatment 2-3 times.

Agricultural prevention and control: Breed disease resistant varieties. Before planting, combine soil preparation and fertilization to maintain good rural hygiene, promptly remove diseased leaves, residual branches, and fruits, and dispose of them centrally. Strengthen training management by applying sufficient basal fertilizer, timely top dressing, or using vegetable-specific fertilizer to ensure nutrient retention during peak fruiting and improve host disease resistance. If seeds are contaminated with bacteria, soak them in 52 ℃ warm water for 30 minutes. Chemical control: Use registered fungicides such as Chlorfenapyr, Propionine Zinc, Tebuconazole, Imidaclopramide, Polymycin, Diclofenac Zinc, Diclofenac Manganese Zinc, with specific dosages of 1125g/ha of 40% chlorothalonil wettable powder, 450g/ha of 500g/l isofungicide suspension, 2250g/ha of 80% mancozeb wettable powder, and 150g/ha of 10% benzofenapyr water dispersible granule, applied by spray. Seed processing: Soak seeds in 2% Wuyi mycin or mix with 0.4% by weight of seeds of 50% wettable powder. Disinfection of solar greenhouse: For greenhouses with a history of disease, perform high-temperature confinement for one week in summer and autumn. Before planting, in a sealed greenhouse, mix 1.5 kg of sulfur and 3 kg of sawdust per acre, light several piles to smoke overnight, or use 45% Bacillus thuringiensis smoke suppressant for fumigation.

Scientific irrigation and fertilization: maintain appropriate soil moisture, avoiding over drying or waterlogging; apply fertilizers reasonably, balancing nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium, avoid excessive nitrogen, and enhance plant disease resistance. Trimming and ventilation: regularly trim branches and leaves to maintain ventilation and light in the tree crown; remove diseased and overly dense branches to reduce pathogen breeding. Health management: timely clean fallen leaves, fruit residues, and surrounding weeds to reduce overwintering and breeding grounds; keep trimming, harvesting, and other tools clean to avoid spreading pathogens. Regular monitoring and observation: check leaves, fruits, and branches weekly to detect early pests and diseases; use insect traps or sticky boards to monitor pest populations and take preventive measures. Biology and preventive control: apply biological agents (e.g., Bacillus subtilis, Rhizopus oryzae) to enhance plant immunity; during high incidence periods, spray low-toxicity preventive pesticides, avoiding healthy leaves. Principles of protecting healthy leaves: prioritize prevention, maintain healthy leaves, avoid blind pesticide use, reduce environmental pollution and pesticide residues, and focus on comprehensive management to maintain plant vitality and disease resistance.

Agricultural prevention and control: Plant disease resistant varieties such as Yuhong 2, Yuanhong, Zhongshu 5, Zhongshu 4, Jiahong, Qiangfeng, and Jiafen 10. Implement crop rotation with cruciferous vegetables for at least 3 years and avoid planting near potatoes. Cultivate disease-free and strong seedlings using soil not previously planted with Solanaceae crops, and utilize nutrient bowls, bags, or plug trays. Strengthen field management with sufficient base fertilizer, formula fertilization, balanced nitrogen application, and increased phosphorus and potassium. Chemical control: Use smoke methods in protected areas and spray fungicides such as 72.2% Prick water solution 800 times, 40% Methomyl copper wettable powder, 72% Kelu wettable powder 400-600 times, 64% Chlorpyrifos wettable powder 500 times, 10% Shigao 1500 times, and 50% Chlorpyrifos 1000 times every 7-10 days for 4-5 sprays. Commonly used pesticides include 58% metalaxyl manganese zinc wettable powder 500 times, 60% disinfectant alum wettable powder 500 times, 75% chlorothalonil wettable powder 600 times, and 50% carbendazim wettable powder 500 times. For stem lesions, use high concentration solutions such as 56% azoxystrobin 1000 times or 50% methylthiophanate wettable powder 200 times, sprayed once every 7-8 days for 2-3 consecutive times. Avoid harvesting fruits within 10 days after pesticide application.

Agricultural prevention and control: Choose disease resistant varieties such as Jiafen 15, Jiafen 16, Jiafen 17, Zhongza 7, Shenfen 3, and Jiahong 15 according to local conditions. Treat seeds before sowing by soaking in 55 ℃ warm water for 30 minutes, rinse with clean water, and dry in the shade. Arrange a three-year crop rotation in greenhouses with melons or other Solanaceae vegetables to eliminate residual bacterial sources. Disinfect greenhouses by fumigating with a mixture of 0.25kg sulfur powder and 0.50kg sawdust per 37m³ space, seal and smoke for 24 hours, or use 45% chlorothalonil at 0.25kg per 110m² for one day and night. Strengthen cultivation management by controlling temperature and humidity, timely ventilation and dehumidification, appropriate watering, reasonable and dense planting, timely pruning, formula fertilization with balanced nitrogen, and increased phosphorus and potassium application. Remove diseased leaves promptly and bury or burn them deeply. Use high-temperature greenhouse treatment on sunny days by heating to 30-33 ℃ for about 2 hours, followed by ventilation and cooling to control pathogens effectively.

Agricultural prevention and control: Soak seeds in 10% trisodium phosphate solution for 20 minutes, rinse with clean water, or perform dry heat disinfection at 70 ℃ for 72 hours. Strengthen management to prevent high temperatures and drought using sunshade nets. Reduce wounds and avoid root damage during planting and field operations. Prevent aphids timely to reduce virus transmission. For viral diseases, use three types of inhibitors: (1) Virus inactivators such as soybean milk or milk diluted 100-200 times, sprayed on tomato plants; Plant virus passivator 912 can be prepared as a paste and sprayed once in the morning and evening during initial and peak fruiting stages. (2) Protective substances like sodium alginate or high-fat film emulsion to form a protective film, sprayed evenly once every 7 days for 2-3 times. (3) Resistance-enhancing substances such as NS-83 antibiotic (mixed fatty acid) 100-fold solution, sprayed three times before and after planting; or virus A wettable powder, antiviral agents, virus purification, Zhiailing emulsion, and other medicaments sprayed once every 7 days for 2-3 consecutive times.

Agricultural prevention and control: Use crop rotation with new soil or sunny beds that have not been planted with Solanaceae vegetables in the past 2 years for seedlings, and promote 2-3 year rotation with non-Solanaceae vegetables to reduce field pathogen sources. Retain and disinfect seeds by selecting disease-free seeds from healthy plants, coating imported seeds with 2.5% fludioxonil suspension (3-4‰ per thousand) before sowing, or soaking seeds in 52 ℃ warm water for 30 minutes, then drying and promoting germination. Cultivation measures include deep ditches and high ridges to prevent waterlogging, reasonable planting to improve ventilation and light transmission, and appropriate use of phosphorus and potassium fertilizers to enhance disease resistance. Clean the field by regularly removing diseased and old leaves, removing remaining diseased plants and weeds after harvest, and deeply plowing the soil. Compost should be fermented at high temperatures and fully decomposed before application. Chemical control: Spray during early disease stages once every 7-10 days, 2-3 consecutive times. Common pesticides include 50% Chlorpyrifos wettable powder (600x), 64% Chlorpyrifos wettable powder (500x), 58% Methomyl · Manganese Zinc wettable powder (500x), 50% mixed sulfur suspension or 40% polysulfide suspension (500x), 50% carbendazim wettable powder (500-1000x), and 25% ammonium copper water solution (500x).

Biological control: Release natural enemies such as predatory mites (e.g., Chilean planthopper mites) or ladybugs to suppress red spider mite populations; use microbial preparations like Beauveria bassiana spray, which kills 98% of adults, nymphs, and eggs while being harmless to pollinators. Chemical control: Apply agents such as avermectin or pyridaben, ensuring spray covers the back of leaves and plant undersides; avoid long-term use of a single drug to prevent resistance and harvest at intervals of more than 20 days after spraying. Physical control: Regulate the environment using high temperature or dry conditions to directly kill mites; use yellow boards to trap adult insects; perform cleaning management by timely removal of weeds and deep plowing to destroy overwintering eggs.

Agricultural prevention and control: Select disease-resistant varieties such as 'Zhe Qiao 7' and 'Su Qi series'; practice crop rotation with non-Solanaceae crops for more than 3 years; deeply plow soil and apply organic fertilizer to promote decay of diseased material; maintain stable soil moisture with drip irrigation; promptly remove and bury or burn diseased plants. Chemical control: Treat seeds by soaking in 55 ℃ warm water for 30 minutes or in a 100-200 fold solution of 20% thiazole zinc suspension for 30 minutes; in early disease stages, spray 72% agricultural streptomycin 3000-4000 times solution and 3% mycotoxin 600-800 times solution every 7-10 days for 2-3 consecutive times, combined with copper preparations such as 77% copper hydroxide 400-500 times solution to enhance effect. Biological control: Release predatory mites such as Neoseiulus cucumeris to control transmission vectors like thrips; use plant-derived extracts such as 0.5% matrine water solution at 800 times dilution or 5% allicin extract for insecticidal and antibacterial effects.

Strengthen field management by thoroughly cleaning weeds and residual plants inside and outside the greenhouse before transplanting; disinfect the greenhouse with trisodium phosphate or formaldehyde and seal for over 3 days; implement crop rotation with non-Solanaceae crops such as cucumber, zucchini, or beans for at least 3 years to reduce virus and pathogen residues. Physical and biological control of tobacco whiteflies includes setting up isolation nets, installing 30-40 mesh insect nets at entrances and ventilation openings, using yellow boards and pots to trap adults, and hanging 1-2 yellow sticky boards every 10 square meters in the nursery; covering greenhouses with thin film during high temperatures to remove residual insects. Chemical control involves monitoring for tobacco whiteflies and applying treatments promptly when detected; mix drugs such as avermectin and psyllid every 5-7 days, and use plant activators like brassinolide, gibberellin, and oligosaccharides to enhance plant immunity, promote healthy growth, and reduce disease incidence.
